title: Vanity under the sun; wisdom and weariness
summary_text: The Teacher opens by declaring that everything under the sun is vapor and that generations come and go while the earth remains. He watches the ceaseless cycles of creation and human labor, yet none of it leaves lasting satisfaction. Even wisdom cannot straighten what is crooked, and greater knowledge often brings greater grief.
key_points: All things are full of weariness, and nothing is truly new under the sun.
The sun rises and sets, but human striving never reaches completion.
The Teacher applied his heart to wisdom and found its limits.
What is crooked cannot be made straight by human effort.

title: Pleasure tested; toil exposed as vanity
summary_text: The Teacher tests laughter, pleasure, projects, wealth, and labor, but each pursuit ends in emptiness. He sees that wisdom is better than folly, yet both the wise man and the fool die and are forgotten. The only good left in life is to receive God's gifts with gratitude, because all toil ultimately rests in God's hands.
key_points: The Teacher pursued joy, wealth, and great works, but all proved vanity.
Wisdom excels folly, yet death levels both.
A person's labor may be left to another who did not work for it.
The best portion is to eat, drink, and receive life as God's gift.

title: A time for everything; fear God
summary_text: The Teacher sets human life within God's ordered seasons, showing that every joy, loss, and labor has its appointed time. God makes all things beautiful in their season, yet people cannot fully grasp his work from beginning to end. Because judgment belongs to God, the proper response is to enjoy present gifts with reverence and humility.
key_points: There is a season for every matter under heaven.
God has made everything beautiful in its time.
God will judge both the righteous and the wicked.
It is good to rejoice in labor and receive life as a gift.

title: Oppression, companionship, and unstable power
summary_text: The Teacher looks at oppression and sees tears with no comforter, revealing how lonely and harsh human systems can be. He then celebrates the value of companionship, where two are better than one and a threefold cord is not quickly broken. The chapter closes by warning that popularity, youth, and power all pass away, so vanity still shadows human success.
key_points: The Teacher saw the tears of the oppressed and the absence of comfort.
Two people can help, warm, and protect one another better than one alone.
A poor and wise youth can surpass an old king who no longer listens.
Crowd favor is unstable and quickly turns to emptiness.
evidence_phrases: better is a poor and wise youth

title: Reverence in worship; wealth and contentment
summary_text: The Teacher moves from public oppression to the worshiper’s posture before God, urging careful words, humble hearing, and restraint in vows. He warns that riches cannot satisfy the appetite of the soul and that money often multiplies worry rather than peace. The chapter ends by showing that true gladness is to enjoy what God gives, not to store up what cannot be kept.
key_points: Approach God with listening rather than empty words.
Do not make vows lightly and then delay fulfilling them.
Loving silver never leaves a person satisfied.
The ability to enjoy food, work, and rest is a gift from God.
evidence_phrases: better that you should not vow

title: Unenjoyed riches and the hunger of desire
summary_text: The Teacher describes the tragedy of wealth, honor, and long life when God does not grant the ability to enjoy them. Even a full house, many children, and a long name cannot satisfy a person whose appetite remains empty. The chapter presses the question of what is truly good, since human desire keeps asking for more than the world can give.
key_points: Riches are a burden if God does not grant the power to enjoy them.
A stillborn child can be more at rest than a life filled with frustration.
Human appetite is never finally satisfied by more possessions.
No one can tell a person what will truly be good for life.

title: Wisdom, moderation, and the fall of man
summary_text: The Teacher compares sorrow and laughter, showing that hard realities often teach more than easy pleasures. He calls for wisdom that is humble, patient, and careful, instead of self-righteousness or reckless wickedness. Yet even wisdom cannot fully solve the mystery of human sin, because God made people upright and they have sought many schemes.
key_points: A good name and a sober heart are better than shallow joy.
The end of a matter is better than its beginning when it teaches wisdom.
Do not be overly righteous or overly wicked.
God made man upright, but people have pursued many schemes.

title: Authority, delay, and reverence before God
summary_text: The Teacher considers kings and courts, urging wise obedience and patience under authority. Though evil is sometimes not punished quickly, he insists that judgment still belongs to God and that the wicked cannot escape forever. In the end, the right posture is not cynicism but fear of God, because human wisdom cannot master all his works.
key_points: Keep the king's command and do not rush into rebellion.
The wise heart knows the right time and judgment.
Because sentence is delayed, the wicked often grow bold.
Human labor cannot fully comprehend God's works.

title: Death comes to all; enjoy life while you can
summary_text: The Teacher faces the hard truth that death comes to the righteous and the wicked alike, and that the dead no longer participate in life under the sun. Since the future is uncertain, he urges cheerful faithfulness in work, love, and daily enjoyments. Wisdom is still precious, even if the world often forgets the wise person who quietly saves a city.
key_points: One event happens to all people, so life must be received as grace.
Enjoy your portion with the wife whom you love.
Do whatever your hand finds to do with all your might.
Wisdom may be forgotten, but it is still better than strength.
evidence_phrases: whatever your hand finds to do

title: Folly in high places and careless words
summary_text: The Teacher shows how a little folly can ruin honor, and how foolish leadership spreads trouble through a land. He contrasts wise speech and skillful labor with the reckless talk of fools who cannot guide themselves. The chapter warns that even private thoughts and words matter, because folly eventually reveals itself in public harm.
key_points: A little folly can spoil the value of wisdom and honor.
Fools talk too much and too carelessly.
Lazy hands create trouble, while skill helps preserve order.
A land suffers when its rulers and servants indulge folly.

title: Generosity, uncertainty, and the call to act
summary_text: The Teacher counsels bold generosity because life is uncertain and outcomes cannot be controlled. He calls people to sow, labor, and do good while they can, instead of waiting for ideal conditions that never arrive. Youth is a gift, but it must be enjoyed with awareness that God will bring every hidden thing into judgment.
key_points: Cast your bread on the waters and keep doing good.
You do not know which work will prosper, so keep working.
Rejoice in youth, but remember that God sees it all.
Every hidden thing will be brought into judgment.
evidence_phrases: God will bring you into judgment

title: Remember your Creator; fear God and keep his commandments
summary_text: The Teacher ends by urging the young to remember their Creator before the weakness of age steals strength, joy, and desire. He pictures the body’s decay in vivid poetic language and reminds the reader that dust returns to dust, just as the spirit returns to God. The book closes by reducing human duty to one clear response: fear God, keep his commandments, and wait for his final judgment.
key_points: Remember your Creator in the days of your youth.
The body will weaken and the grave will finally claim it.
The dust returns to the earth and the spirit returns to God.
Fear God and keep his commandments, for he will judge every work.
evidence_phrases: fear God and keep his commandments
